sources.notes: Statement and constraints: answer range queries about maximizing inversions after replacing each element by its prefix maximum or prefix minimum within the subarray.
Used to confirm block decomposition by equal prefix-min, concave quadratic optimization inside each block, and binary lifting over next-smaller blocks.
Referenced as a community video editorial for the greedy idea; not used for implementation details.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution page; not used as evidence in this review.

annotation.summary: For each subarray query, choose for every position either the subarray prefix maximum or prefix minimum to maximize the number of inversions.
annotation.constraints: n and q are large, so each query must use preprocessed block jumps rather than scanning every element.
annotation.core_idea: Within a query, positions with the same prefix minimum form a block, and choices from different blocks are independent. If s elements precede a block of length t and x of this block are chosen as prefix maximum before the rest are prefix minimum, the contribution is (s+x)(t-x), a concave quadratic maximized near (t-s)/2. Blocks with t <= s are simple and can be jumped in batches; blocks with t > s can occur only O(log n) times because the processed prefix length at least doubles. Precompute next-smaller positions with a monotonic stack, then use binary lifting and prefix sums of block lengths/squares to skip many simple blocks per query.
annotation.complexity: O((n+q) log n) intended, with O(n log n + q log^2 n) also acceptable.
tricks: partition query by equal prefix-min blocks
optimize a concave quadratic per block
large blocks can occur only logarithmically often

solution_variants.name: next-smaller-block-binary-lifting
solution_variants.summary: Build next-smaller blocks with a monotonic stack, precompute binary-lifting jumps and block-length sums, then answer each query by jumping over type-2 blocks and handling O(log n) large blocks directly.

solution_variants.name: fenwick-active-blocks
solution_variants.summary: Alternative offline implementation maintains active block contributions with Fenwick trees while processing left endpoints.

tags.evidence: Next-smaller positions define the equal-prefix-min blocks and are computed with a monotonic stack.
The query algorithm jumps over many simple blocks using binary lifting over next-smaller block links.
Inside each block, the optimal form is to choose a prefix of prefix-max choices followed by prefix-min choices.
Each block reduces to maximizing the single-variable concave quadratic (s+x)(t-x).
A Fenwick tree appears in an alternative offline implementation, but the main reviewed solution uses binary lifting.
